title: Technological Headroom: How System Configuration Shapes the Value of Additional Team Members
data_source: Hospital robotic surgery: 836 cases (2012-2015), 52 direct procedure observations, 32 interviews, 19 months ethnographic fieldwork

categories.framing: Adding trainees to surgical teams has variable cost depending on technology configuration
categories.mechanism: Console configuration (single vs dual) determines whether trainees can take productive roles
categories.evidence: 836 robotic surgery cases with timing data; 4x cost difference: +100 min (single console) vs +22 min (dual console) for adding 2nd trainee
categories.claims: Technology configuration creates or eliminates productive roles for additional team members
categories.architecture: Standard mixed-methods: quant interaction effect + qual mechanism

events.summary: Initial exploration of trainee cost variation in robotic surgery
events.detail: Applied theory-forge pipeline to dormant hospital ethnography data. Found striking 4x interaction: adding a second trainee costs +100 minutes in single-console rooms but only +22 minutes in dual-console rooms. Qualitative fieldwork (52 observations, 32 interviews) reveals mechanism: dual console creates productive trainee roles (proctored operation + dedicated bedside), while single console forces competition for limited action possibilities.
events.trigger: Theory-forge pipeline applied to dormant data from robotic surgery ethnography

categories.mechanism: Theory identified: Process Losses (Steiner 1972) extended with technology affordances boundary condition

events.summary: Theory development: Process Losses + Technology Affordances
events.detail: Identified primary theory violated: Process Losses/Coordination Costs (Steiner 1972). Extended with Gibson (1979) and Leonardi (2011) affordance theory. Key insight: coordination costs are not inherent to team size — they depend on whether technology affords productive roles for additional members.

categories.evidence: 35 supporting quotes, 2 challenging. Smoking gun: 'I can't teach you and this is not a dual console. Take your head out.'

events.detail: Mined 32 interview transcripts and 52 observation field notes. Found 35 supporting evidence items, 2 challenging. Key quotes: surgeon explicitly refusing to teach in single-console room; dual-console described as enabling concurrent training; bedside role teaching captured in detail.

categories.validation: Claims audit: 35 supporting, 2 challenging, 0 high-concern claims

events.summary: Claims audit completed: strong evidence base
events.detail: Adversarial audit of all claims against qualitative evidence. 35 supporting items, 2 challenging, 0 high-concern. Evidence base is unusually strong due to direct ethnographic observation of the mechanism in action.

categories.framing: Named concept: 'Technological Headroom' — the capacity of a technology configuration to afford productive roles for additional participants
categories.mechanism: Dual console creates headroom (productive trainee roles); single console creates congestion (pure coordination loss)
categories.architecture: Full manuscript: Abstract, Intro, Theory, Methods, Findings, Discussion
categories.validation: Becker generalization PASS; Genre evaluation PASS (fixed hypo-deductive framing issue)

events.summary: Full manuscript drafted with named concept
events.detail: Complete manuscript drafted at ~11,500 words. Named concept 'technological headroom' coined to describe the capacity of technology configurations to afford productive roles. Becker generalization test passed. Genre evaluation passed after fixing hypo-deductive framing. Bibliography has 9 citations (likely needs expansion for OrgSci).
